title: Research Question & Literature Review - Fill in the Blank

questions.question: Name the predicted answer to a research question.
questions.blank_sentence: While the research question is the question itself, the ___ is your predicted answer to it.
questions.explanation: The RQ is the question; the hypothesis is the predicted answer to that question.

questions.question: Name the citation mistake of citing a paper that cites the original.
questions.blank_sentence: Citing a paper that cites the original instead of the original itself is called citing the ___.
questions.explanation: "Citing the citation" means citing a secondary source that references the original; you should always track down and cite the original source for a definition or finding.

questions.question: Name the classic phenomenon Cherry (1953) studied.
questions.blank_sentence: Cherry's 1953 study of the ___-party effect is an example of a sharply scoped research question from a real phenomenon.
questions.explanation: The cocktail-party effect (Cherry, 1953) is cited as a sharply scoped RQ drawn from a real, observable phenomenon.

questions.question: Name the framing (Belcher 2019) for how your paper enters a scholarly debate.
questions.blank_sentence: Belcher (2019) describes writing as joining the ___, where your paper enters an ongoing scholarly debate.
questions.explanation: "Joining the conversation" frames your work as entering an ongoing scholarly debate rather than standing alone.

questions.question: Name the trap where a review just describes each source instead of analysing.
questions.blank_sentence: A review that describes one source after another instead of comparing them falls into the ___ effect.
questions.explanation: The summary effect is avoided by grouping studies into sub-themes or sides of a debate rather than summarising each source in turn.

questions.question: Name what GenAI models do when they fabricate non-existent references.
questions.blank_sentence: The course warns against fully GenAI-written text because models ___ references that do not exist.
questions.explanation: Generative AI models hallucinate references and content, which is the main reason the course discourages fully GenAI-generated text.

questions.question: Name the review property that means it groups by topic, not author.
questions.blank_sentence: Sources should be organised by topic or ___, not by author, to make comparison easier.
questions.explanation: Organising by topic or purpose (into sub-themes) facilitates comparison and guides the reader toward your research gap.

questions.question: Name the feasibility constraint of running a study within one semester.
questions.blank_sentence: The feasibility filter asks whether time, participants, equipment, and ethical ___ are realistic within one semester.
questions.explanation: Feasibility means checking that time, participants, equipment, and ethical approval are all realistic within the available semester.

questions.question: Name the review quality the course says it must be, not a summary.
questions.blank_sentence: A literature review is not a summary; it is critical ___ tied to your project.
questions.explanation: A lit review is analysis and critical evaluation linked to your project, showing relevance, the gap, and why your RQ matters.

questions.question: Name one reference-manager tool used for LaTeX bibliographies.
questions.blank_sentence: For managing references in LaTeX documents, the format ___ is commonly used alongside tools like Zotero and Mendeley.
questions.explanation: BibTeX is the reference format used with LaTeX, while Zotero and Mendeley are general-purpose reference managers.
